Added a negative branch-only proof fixture and the ordinary Haxe span[index] = condition ? a : b positive/native case. Verified registered span snapshot update/full suite (54 requests; strict native and ASan/UBSan), npm run test:hxc-ir, and the Caxecraft Eval/native/sanitizer water lane.
close_reason: Closed

title: Clean stale content inside owned Caxecraft play variants
description: The content-layout migration left the formerly generated scenarios/first-playable/messages.json in an existing launcher-owned build variant. stage_content_catalogs rejected it as foreign even though the verified parent output marker proves the bounded variant belongs to the launcher, preventing normal game startup.
acceptance_criteria: Owned play variants remove exact stale staged files and now-empty directories before copying the current content set. Unmarked destinations still reject unexpected files without deleting them. The focused localization/package test reproduces both cases, and a real build-only launcher run succeeds from the previously affected variant.
close_reason: Closed

title: Admit transient borrowed spans on final-class instance methods
description: Caxecraft water exposed an over-broad haxe.c restriction: every instance method parameter whose type lowers to a borrowed span is rejected, even when the declaring class is final and the span is used only during that direct call. Admit this bounded direct-call case without allowing the span in constructors, fields, returns, virtual/overridable methods, generic specializations, or recursive escape paths. Preserve the existing virtual-span negative diagnostic.
acceptance_criteria: A focused positive fixture passes a mutable or const borrowed span through a method on a final class and runs warning-clean native C. Existing virtual-method, constructor, field, return, recursive, and generic span negatives remain fail-closed. The compiler change is generic and contains no Caxecraft special case.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integrate authored water with packs, maps, rendering, editor, and saves
description: Expose the proven water mechanics through the validated content pack, CaxeMap/CaxeFlow, editor, renderer/audio/HUD, and versioned world/save state. Author the Adventure water route and Tideweave placement as data only after the generic mechanics exist.
design: Extend only the smallest closed schemas earned by the implemented mechanics. The pack declares the water medium/render profile and equipment capability; CaxeMap owns sources/initial volumes; saves own mutable fluid queue/state and player oxygen/equipment. Renderer reads simulation state and never advances it.
acceptance_criteria: Built-in water and Tideweave definitions validate; editor authors sources and volumes; canonical map/save round trips preserve fluid state; above/below-surface visuals, audio, HUD, loading, and real-raylib pilot pass; Adventure placement has no content-specific Haxe branches.
notes:
Checkpoint 2026-07-21: schema/data/editor slice implemented. Built-in content schema 2 now validates water as a fluid, ordinary/Tideweave aquatic profiles, and the Tideweave suit without treating water as terrain. CAXEMAP 1 now parses, validates, canonically writes, and editor undo/redo authors permanent sources plus finite initial volumes; first-playable data owns its water and Tideweave placement. Focused scenario/editor/content checks, formatter, all-sources, typed-boundaries, and snapshot catalog pass. Native loading, mutable save state, rendering/audio/HUD, and real-Raylib pilot remain under this open task.
Water/save integration checkpoint: added transactional finite initial-volume loading, canonical in-memory water snapshot capture/restore, exact pending-queue reconstruction, and Eval/generated-C continuation parity under strict C and sanitizers. Full CAXESAVE file/container state remains correctly owned by haxe_c-4my. Compiler pressure also found duplicate enum-switch exit HXC9000 and recorded general bug haxe_c-7bw; application validation now uses centralized primitive byte predicates, which is the cleaner codec boundary.

title: Blend submersion, swimming, buoyancy, breath, and Tideweave
description: Build player-medium behavior on the deterministic water field. Sample feet/body/head immersion, derive a continuous submersion ratio and stable named movement state, then blend gravity, buoyancy, drag, acceleration, ascent/descent, floating, breaching, camera effects, oxygen, drowning, recovery, and the bounded Tideweave capability without waterline jitter.
design: Physics owns truth at the fixed simulation tick. Rendering interpolates positions and presentation only. Sample several documented heights across the player AABB, apply hysteresis around state labels, and derive forces from the measured fraction instead of one Boolean water flag. Keep suit effects as a typed capability/profile supplied by validated content, not a campaign-name branch.
acceptance_criteria: Focused deterministic traces cover dry entry, wading, surface floating, full submersion, ascent/descent, breach, exit, oxygen loss/recovery, drowning/retry, and equipped/unequipped Tideweave behavior with no waterline jitter. Eval/C parity and readable runtime-free C pass.
close_reason: Implemented fixed-tick continuous submersion, stable dry/wading/floating/submerged labels, shared land/water collision, swimming forces, buoyancy, drag, ascent/descent, breath recovery, drowning cadence, retry reset, and generic bounded equipment capabilities. The Haxe-owned AquaticsProbe passes Eval/native-C trace parity, strict runtime-free C, no-goto output checks, and available ASan/UBSan. Documentation explicitly defers Raylib presentation, content resolution, authored maps, editor, and persistence to haxe_c-xge.20.5.3.

title: Implement deterministic bounded voxel-water flow
description: Add the target-neutral Haxe simulation core for Minecraft-like water. A voxel has a typed empty/source/flowing state with bounded level and falling status; fixed-tick updates prefer downward flow, then deterministic horizontal neighbors, leak through openings, settle, recede, and reflow after solid edits. Keep a bounded work queue so a large release continues across ticks without stalling rendering. This slice owns domain storage, update scheduling, terrain invalidation hooks, stable hashing/traces, and focused Eval/generated-C tests; it does not yet own swimming, breath, rendering, persistence, or authored campaign placement.
design: Use the existing compact one-byte world carrier. Reserve explicit water codes that encode source versus flowing strength/falling state and keep all decoding behind typed Haxe functions; gameplay must never compare magic codes. Use a fixed neighbor order and duplicate-safe bounded queue. Compute a cell's next state from the previous committed neighborhood or another explicitly documented deterministic schedule, never renderer traversal order. World solid placement/removal schedules the changed cell plus bounded neighbors. Per-tick processed-cell budget is observable in tests.
acceptance_criteria: Focused tests prove source stability, downward fall, lateral weakening, one-cell leaks, obstacle routing, stable settling, receding after source removal, dam open/close/repair reflow, deterministic state/hash across enqueue orders and render-frame grouping, and bounded continuation across ticks. Invalid water levels/codes fail closed at the typed boundary. Eval and haxe.c produce the same trace; generated strict C is warning-clean, sanitizer-clean where available, runtime-free, and contains no content-name special cases or raw C.
notes: Implemented target-neutral typed bounded water, compact explicit storage codes, deterministic lowest-index pending work, terrain edit invalidation, bounded tick/settle behavior, and Haxe-owned executable assertions. The shared host runner now coordinates Eval, haxe.c, strict native C, runtime-plan inspection, and optional sanitizers without reimplementing water in Python. Verified test:caxecraft-water (Eval/native exact agreement, runtime-free, ASan/UBSan), typed boundaries, all owned Haxe sources, Haxe format, CI/toolchain policy, and snapshot catalog.
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement water, swimming, breath, and Tideweave underwater play
description: Deliver water as a real simulated voxel fluid used by Creative mode and the authored Adventure route. Water has typed source and flowing cell states, falls through open space, leaks through openings, spreads laterally with bounded strength, recedes or settles after neighboring blocks change, and schedules deterministic local updates when water or terrain is placed or removed. Player physics measures submersion continuously and transitions through wading, surface swimming, floating, fully underwater movement, and breaching: effective gravity, buoyancy, drag, acceleration, jump/swim controls, camera, breath, and audio/visual presentation respond to that measured state. Add breath depletion/recovery, drowning damage, respawn/checkpoint behavior, underwater mining policy, and the Tideweave Suit capability. Geometry, suit placement, vault/clue progression, flooded passages, hazards, and route gates remain CaxeMap/CaxeFlow/content-pack data; reusable Haxe systems must not recognize named campaign locations or characters.
design: Use a small deterministic Minecraft-like cellular model rather than a general fluid solver. Each water cell records a closed state such as source or a bounded flow level plus falling state. Updates prefer downward flow, then recompute lateral spread from stable neighbors; a deterministic queue and fixed neighbor order make the same edits produce the same world and save data on every target. Block placement/removal invalidates only a bounded neighboring region, while per-tick work has an explicit budget so a large leak cannot freeze rendering. Define source renewal, decay, chunk/level boundary, unloaded-region, and save/load rules explicitly; water must never duplicate from iteration order or disappear because rendering culled it. Player physics samples feet/body/head submersion and blends effective gravity, buoyancy, drag, and control across the surface, with small stable thresholds to prevent jitter. Rendering interpolates but never owns fluid or oxygen truth. Keep water cells distinct from air and solids. Defer lava, pressure, pumps, mixing chemistry, and waterlogging unless authored content proves they are needed. All owned behavior is Haxe compiled through haxe.c; raylib externs remain presentation-only.
acceptance_criteria: Typed source/flow states are validated and cannot be confused with solid or passable-air content; water falls, spreads sideways, leaks through a one-cell opening, flows around obstacles, reaches a stable bounded state, and recomputes after a dam is built, opened, or repaired; deterministic fixed-tick traces and save round trips prove identical cell states across iteration order and varying render frame times; update budgets prevent a large release from hanging a frame and continue simulation on later ticks. Player entry/exit, wading, surface float, full submersion, underwater ascent/descent, smooth gravity/buoyancy/drag transitions, surface breaching, breath depletion/recovery, warnings, drowning, death/retry, and checkpoint restoration are deterministic and do not jitter at the waterline. Equipping the validated Tideweave Suit grants its documented bounded air/swimming/underwater-mining/cold-water behavior and save/load preserves it. Missing suit, depleted air, unsafe re-entry, suit acquisition, and the alternate bridge route cannot soft-lock progression. CaxeMap/CaxeFlow/editor data authors water sources, initial volumes, suit and route gates without content-specific engine branches. Water is visually legible above and below the surface and essential state is not color/audio-only. Eval/generated-C traces, malformed/wrong-kind pack tests, CaxeTest scripts, real-raylib pilots, strict native compilers, sanitizers, draw/update/allocation budgets, and readable generated C pass. No lava, pressure, chemistry, or arbitrary fluid claim is made.

comments.author: Marcelo Serpa
comments.text: Scope strengthened from static bounded volumes to deterministic Minecraft-like cellular flow at maintainer request: leaking, downward/lateral propagation, reflow after edits, and continuous submersion-based gravity/buoyancy transitions are now required behavior rather than future work.

title: Generalize CaxeFlow event sources and visual trigger authoring
description:
## Why

Creators need one understandable way to make world events cause any admitted engine behavior. A collision volume that can only launch a cutscene, or a separate special-case system for doors, music, quests, and encounters, would fragment the engine and editor.

## What

Generalize CaxeFlow around typed event sources, optional predicates, and ordered actions. Spatial trigger volumes are one source alongside interaction, object, actor, block, quest, timer, level, and engine lifecycle events. Every source carries a closed validated context, and every action remains an admitted typed engine capability. Cutscenes, dialogue, bridge movement, enemy waves, weather, audio, objectives, level transitions, and reusable sequences are action choices rather than distinct trigger systems.

## How

Preserve the existing deterministic fixed-tick executor and deferred-event boundary. Define event adapters in typed Haxe, stable event IDs and context fields in the shared registry, explicit repeat/cooldown/per-actor policy, half-open spatial boundaries, ordering for overlapping volumes, and behavior for spawn, teleport, fast movement, disable, save, and reload. The editor places and resizes visible trigger volumes, uses icon-and-text WHEN/IF/DO cards, obtains valid fields/actions from the shared registry, previews event flow, and test-plays through the real engine. Advanced mode exposes the same data with nested conditions, event context, sequences, and signals; it is not a second language. No content can register arbitrary code or a host callback.
design: Use one registry-backed event-to-action architecture. An event adapter observes an authoritative engine fact and emits a closed event value. Rules read a stable tick snapshot, filter with typed predicates, and enqueue admitted world or presentation actions. New mechanics enter through small reviewed Haxe adapters plus registry schema, validator, exhaustive executor ownership, editor metadata, and tests. Presentation completion such as an audio device finishing a sound must not be the sole authority for required world progress. Avoid a universal dynamic event bus, string-key payload maps, per-feature trigger classes, and an embedded general-purpose scripting language.
acceptance_criteria: The documented model and shared typed registry distinguish event sources, event context, conditions, actions, and deferred follow-up events; spatial sources cover enter and leave with deterministic actor filtering, overlap order, boundary, spawn-inside, teleport or swept-movement, disable, repeat, cooldown, save, and reload semantics; required Adventure events can start any admitted action or reusable sequence, including a cutscene, without a special trigger implementation; the visual editor can place, select, resize, name, connect, copy, validate, and test trigger volumes and can build readable WHEN/IF/DO cards using world picking rather than raw IDs; an event-flow overlay and bounded trace show which source fired, why a predicate passed or failed, which actions ran, and which follow-up events were deferred; Advanced mode exposes the same canonical CaxeFlow data and event context rather than a second format; malformed context, wrong-kind references, event cycles, overlapping ambiguity, stale references, unsupported actions, and work-budget exhaustion fail with plain English and es-MX-ready diagnostics; Eval, generated C, save/reload, editor round-trip, and selected native pilots prove deterministic parity; arbitrary code, dynamic payload maps, host paths, raw input keys, wall-clock events, and presentation-device completion as required gameplay authority remain rejected.

comments.author: Marcelo Serpa
comments.text: Authoring-depth contract: Guided cards, Advanced visual logic, and Text are lossless views of the same typed CaxeMap/CaxeFlow draft. Text mode uses the bounded data DSL with registry-backed completion, canonical formatting, exact diagnostics, and jump-to-world references; it has no privileged actions or arbitrary code authority. Switching back to cards must represent every admitted construct without flattening it into an opaque custom-script node. Invalid text remains a draft and cannot replace the last playable scenario. This path should be comfortable for expert modders and automation agents while preserving the child-friendly entry point.

title: Admit managed Array values in private Haxe enum payloads
description: CaxeFlow Schedule, CallSequence, and ChooseSeeded carry typed Haxe arrays inside FlowAction payloads. The native array seed exists, but haxe.c does not yet lower general Haxe Array values or give managed references a sound enum-payload ownership and lifetime policy. Add that integration without replacing typed action payloads with raw handles or application-only numeric tables.
design: Begin with a minimized enum carrying Array<Int>, then a nested element type representative of FlowArgument and FlowChoice. Reuse the representation, element specialization, mutation, bounds, allocation, tracing or explicit lifetime, and runtime-feature decisions owned by haxe_c-7d0. Enum construction and projection must preserve reference identity and array aliasing; copying an enum must not duplicate or prematurely release the array. Do not hide arrays behind void pointers, unconditional boxing, raw C, or a Caxecraft special case.
acceptance_criteria: Minimized Array<Int> and one closed typed nested-array enum payload compile through validated HxcIR and structural C; construction, enum copy, exhaustive match, checked projection, mutation visibility, empty/nonempty arrays, and cleanup match Eval and the collection contract; ownership, root/lifetime, specialization, allocation, and selected runtime features are explicit in plans; unsupported element or lifetime cases fail before output; split/package/unity bytes are deterministic and generated C is readable, warning-clean, sanitizer-clean, and native-tested with GCC and Clang; array, enum, runtime-plan, and reduced CaxeFlow fixtures pass without public ABI or universal boxing claims.

title: Admit immutable String values in private Haxe enum payloads
description: Caxecraft uses a typed FlowAction enum carrying nominal ScenarioId and ContentId abstracts whose runtime representation is Haxe String. Eval accepts this ordinary model, but haxe.c currently stops at HXC1001 while planning the enum payload, reporting reference-String-non-null. Admit the narrow immutable String carrier selected by the owning String analysis instead of forcing the application to erase nominal IDs or replace the typed enum with integers.
design: Start from a minimal enum with one abstract-over-String payload constructed from a literal, passed through a function, exhaustively matched, and projected. Extend private enum layout planning only after String representation, lifetime, and runtime-feature selection are explicit. Preserve the abstract's Haxe identity in diagnostics/plans while storing the resolved String representation in HxcIR/C. Do not treat arbitrary references as pointers, freeze a public ABI, assume every String is a literal, or make the enum lowering own general String operations. Coordinate with haxe_c-c3s and the existing string-literal carrier.
acceptance_criteria: A minimized abstract-over-String enum payload compiles from typed AST through validated HxcIR and structural C; literal and nonliteral admitted String sources use the representation/lifetime selected by the String owner; construction, copy, exhaustive match, checked projection, equality where admitted, and failure paths match Eval; unsupported ownership or operation cases fail before artifact emission with source-positioned diagnostics; split/package/unity output is deterministic, warning-clean, readable, and selects only justified runtime features; strict GCC/Clang C and C++ consumer/native evidence, sanitizer coverage where ownership applies, runtime plan, generated layout assertions, enum snapshots, String tests, and Caxecraft's reduced ScenarioId case pass; no public ABI or full String parity is claimed.

title: Define the minimal typed Caxecraft content-pack manifest
description: Turn the existing CAXEMAP asset-pack logical path into a real validated package boundary. Today the path round-trips, ScenarioContentRegistry is only an interface supplied by handwritten Haxe, and assets/manifest.json owns visual provenance and packaged bytes rather than general blocks, items, actors, behaviors, drops, or presentation definitions. Define only the manifest needed to instantiate the first playable without content-specific engine code.
design: Keep CAXEMAP focused on one level and let it reference one stable pack ID or logical package path. A small versioned data-only pack manifest defines logical content IDs and their closed kinds, bounded mechanic/profile selections, numeric parameters, drop references, and logical asset/message/effect references. Reuse the existing reviewed asset manifest for byte identity and provenance instead of duplicating file hashes. Parse untyped JSON only in one validated boundary and immediately convert to closed Haxe types; a generated typed adapter may embed the validated built-in pack until native JSON/filesystem support exists. Packs select registered engine capabilities but cannot embed Haxe, C, callbacks, reflection, shell commands, host paths, or arbitrary scripts. Do not design a general mod marketplace, dependency solver, inheritance system, or universal game schema.
acceptance_criteria: A short format reference distinguishes the current visual asset manifest, the new content manifest, CaxeMap level data, campaign data, and save overlays; packs have stable ID/version, exact closed content kinds, deterministic canonical validation, bounded inventories, and collision-safe content IDs; the built-in base pack defines every block, item, NPC/enemy actor, behavior/profile, combat/stat parameter, drop, terrain/render profile, and effect needed by the first playable without duplicating visual hashes or localized story prose; CaxeMap asset-pack resolves to one validated pack and every referenced content ID is checked for the required kind; duplicate/unknown/wrong-kind IDs, missing registered mechanics, invalid bounds, traversal/host paths, cycles where disallowed, malformed JSON, and stale generated adapters fail closed; clean packaging copies the exact reviewed manifest and asset bytes; editor queries use the same typed definitions; Eval and generated-C traces, deterministic repeated/package builds, malformed corpus, typed-boundary checks, native playable pilots, and extraction documentation pass; capability docs state that content packs are planned until this gate closes.
close_reason: Implemented schema-1 built-in content.json, strict deterministic validator, generated typed Haxe adapter and ScenarioContentRegistry, first-playable and editor resolution, exact runtime packaging, representative malformed/wrong-kind tests, Eval oracle, runtime-free strict C, CI wiring, and evidence-bounded creator/architecture docs. General runtime pack discovery/loading remains deferred under the parent composition work.

comments.author: Marcelo Serpa
comments.text: Boundary clarification from the Inventory.starter sweep: the content registry defines validated item identities and item facts, but it should not silently own one campaign start quantity. Starting loadouts and later grants are authored progression/setup and should be invoked through the shared CaxeFlow action model using resolved item IDs. Inventory Haxe owns only reusable mechanics. The loader/composition path must validate referenced items and bounds before a live session is created.

comments.author: Marcelo Serpa
comments.text: Water is required by the authored Adventure and now has a dedicated implemented-mechanics owner, haxe_c-xge.20.5. Do not add caxecraft:water to the base pack as decorative data during this minimal current-playable slice. After the generic fluid/player-medium mechanics exist, schema 1 or its reviewed successor should admit a closed fluid block policy, Tideweave equipment capability, water render profile, and typed effects; haxe_c-xge.20.5 then extends the pack and CaxeMap fixtures with wrong-kind and gameplay parity evidence.

title: Centralize the explicit CaxeFlow action descriptors
description: Create the first reviewable action-registry foundation from the actions CaxeFlow already executes. Stable syntax IDs, closed argument roles, authority families, editor labels/help keys, canonical trace shape, parser admission, and writer spelling must come from one typed descriptor inventory instead of drifting across independent switches. Preserve the existing strongly typed FlowAction enum and ordinary engine execution semantics.
design: Use module-level functions and closed enums/records. The registry is immutable capability metadata, not a global service locator and not a callback container. Keep FlowAction constructors because they give Haxe compile-time payload checking. Move stable external names and schemas into explicit descriptors; let parser/writer/editor/trace consumers query those facts. Execution remains owned by CaxeFlowExecutor and exhaustive over FlowAction. Reject duplicate IDs, missing constructor coverage, forbidden consumer authority, and descriptor/action shape mismatches. Do not add a macro: first measure the explicit descriptor boilerplate across real consumers.
acceptance_criteria: Every admitted FlowAction constructor has exactly one stable descriptor with a unique CAXEMAP spelling, world/presentation/control family, closed ordered argument roles, authorized consumers, editor label/help message IDs, and deterministic trace name; parser admission and canonical writer spelling use that descriptor inventory instead of separate string literals; an editor-facing palette query returns only actions authorized for CaxeFlow and has deterministic order; coverage tests fail for duplicate IDs, missing descriptors, wrong argument roles, forbidden consumer access, unknown syntax, and descriptor/action mismatch; all existing CAXEMAP fixtures round-trip byte-identically and CaxeFlow semantic traces remain unchanged; Eval, generated-C, native, formatting, typed-boundary, scenario-model, editor, and determinism gates pass; the implementation contains no Dynamic, reflection, runtime registration, arbitrary callbacks, macro, service locator, or weakened exhaustive FlowAction execution.
notes: Generated-C audit on 2026-07-21: compiling ScenarioModelProbe through the production custom target reaches the ordinary FlowAction ShowDialogue payload and fails closed at CaxeFlow.hx:87 with HXC1001, reference-String-non-null, before output. FlowAction also has Array-backed Schedule, CallSequence, and ChooseSeeded payloads. Eval/model/editor/runtime/determinism evidence passes, but generated-C/native closure is not valid until haxe_c-2ph and haxe_c-45z establish sound String and Array enum payload representations. Do not work around this by erasing nominal IDs, replacing FlowAction with integers, raw C, target conditionals, or claiming unrelated DomainProbe C output as registry evidence.

title: Instantiate Caxecraft gameplay from CaxeMap and content packs
description: Remove the handwritten first-playable content currently duplicated across World, Main, GuideNpc, Mossling, combat, drops, actor rendering, and HUD selection. A validated level and its content pack must be the editable source for terrain, placements, actor types, dialogue, rewards, encounter wiring, presentation profiles, and player-visible message references. Reusable Haxe systems load that data into generic runtime instances and execute registered mechanics. This is a product architecture correction and a compiler QA slice, not a request for a general ECS, runtime reflection, or arbitrary scripting.
design: Introduce the smallest typed level-to-runtime composition boundary. Use real classes for stateful runtime sessions or actor instances when identity and lifetime make them clearer, composition rather than one subclass per character, and module functions for stateless transitions. CaxeMap owns stable object IDs, transforms, tags, dialogue/objective references, and initial state. CaxeFlow owns gifts, drops, spawning, state changes, signals, and progression through the shared typed action registry. A validated content-pack registry owns item, block, actor, behavior, render-profile, animation, audio, and drop-table IDs. Haxe owns bounded reusable movement, interaction, combat, inventory, AI, dialogue, and rendering mechanics. Generated adapters may embed validated content until native loading exists, but Main and gameplay code must not recreate that content. If a suitable ordinary-Haxe class shape exposes a compiler limitation, reduce it to a focused compiler fixture and fix or track the general capability instead of reverting to content-specific records and static helpers.
acceptance_criteria: A checked-in audit classifies every content-specific name or value under Caxecraft source as authored data, generated adapter, reusable mechanic, product-global UI/theme, or justified target adapter; World.prepareSpawnMeadow, hardcoded actor start coordinates, GuideNpc and GuidePhase, Mossling-specific combat/drop coupling, and character-name branches in Main are removed; the first playable is instantiated from its validated CaxeMap plus content-pack definitions through one typed composition path; Nia placement, dialogue, interaction stages, gift, and messages plus Mossling placement, behavior selection, stats, attack, defeat, drop, sprites, and feedback are selected by stable content IDs and CaxeFlow rather than duplicated Haxe story code; inventory, combat targets, drops, terrain materials, and actor rendering consume generic typed definitions without stringly typed dispatch; stateful runtime values use clear class instances where appropriate and generated C retains readable structs, typed functions, direct calls, bounded storage, and measured allocation/runtime choices; unknown IDs, wrong content kinds, missing behavior or presentation profiles, duplicate placements, and invalid rule references fail before replacing the live session; Eval and generated-C semantic parity, canonical map/package round trips, save-compatible stable IDs, deterministic traces, focused negative tests, native pilots, playable snapshots, strict compilers, sanitizers, and generated-C maintainability gates pass; test fixtures and application paths contain no character, map, or path special case.
notes: Reality check 2026-07-21: CAXEMAP 1 currently round-trips only an asset-pack logical path; ScenarioContentRegistry is an implemented typed interface but its concrete facts are handwritten adapters, and assets/manifest.json is a real reviewed visual-provenance/package manifest rather than a general game-content schema. Child haxe_c-xge.20.4.1 now owns the minimal typed content manifest/loader. Scoped agent rules and the game-design document must call packs planned until that child closes. The first vertical runtime refactor may consume the existing compile-time registry/generated adapter, but cannot claim a shipped pack system.

comments.author: Marcelo Serpa
comments.text: Targeted authored-content coupling sweep (2026-07-21): Inventory.starter hardcodes the first playable loadout and Main selects it directly. This must become validated authored level/campaign setup, expressed through the shared CaxeFlow action path (for example bounded inventory grant/set actions) and resolved item IDs; Inventory keeps only stacking, capacity, selection, transfer, and consumption mechanics. The same sweep confirmed the existing issue also owns Main spawn coordinates, guide/Mossling construction, berry gift/drop wiring, named messages and sprites; World.prepareSpawnMeadow; GuideNpc/GuidePhase; BerryDrop.fromDefeatedMossling; Mossling-specific profile constants/state; CaxecraftAtlas named actor/item mappings; and TitleMenu coupling to FirstPlayableCatalog. ItemKind and InventoryState also encode the first fixed item roster and should consume the planned typed content registry once compiler collection prerequisites permit it. Product-wide mechanics such as the eight-slot limit, maximum stack, input mapping, and generic inventory operations are not authored campaign content and may remain Haxe. Pilot-only setup may define explicit fixture overrides, but must derive its normal baseline through the same loaded authored content path. Remove these as vertical slices under this issue; do not add separate character/path conditionals.

title: Create one extensible typed action registry for CaxeFlow, cutscenes, console, and CaxeTest
description:
## Why

Adding a gameplay capability such as entering a vehicle must not require unrelated hand-maintained switches in the CaxeFlow parser, editor, cutscene player, console, and QA driver. These consumers share vocabulary, but they do not share authority: authored rules perform game actions, cutscenes sequence game and presentation actions, while tests normally drive player input and observe outcomes.

## What

Define the smallest typed, inspectable registry that lets an engine subsystem register a stable action ID, closed typed arguments, validation, execution authority, editor description, localization keys, and deterministic trace shape once. Use that registry to expose admitted actions to CaxeFlow text, visual cards, cutscene sequences, the debug console, and CaxeTest where each consumer is authorized. Keep player inputs, authored world actions, cinematic presentation commands, read-only observations, test assertions, and explicit test/cheat setup as distinct typed families even when one engine driver carries them.

## How

Start with explicit Haxe descriptors and handlers so the contract is reviewable. After two or more real action families expose repeated mechanical declarations, evaluate a narrow compile-time macro that derives descriptors from annotated typed Haxe declarations. The macro is optional tooling, not runtime reflection or arbitrary-code exposure; generated facts must remain typed, deterministic, inspectable, source-positioned, and independently validated.
design: Use one registry of closed action descriptors with one execution owner per action. Treat the registry as capability metadata, not a service locator: engine systems retain their state and behavior. Separate command families by authority: PlayerInput, WorldAction, PresentationAction, Observation, Assertion, and Setup/Cheat. CaxeFlow can invoke admitted WorldAction values; cutscenes sequence admitted WorldAction and PresentationAction values; CaxeTest usually emits PlayerInput and Assertion values, with visibly classified Setup/Cheat only when requested; the console exposes only commands enabled by the current build/session. Prefer explicit descriptors first. Admit a macro only for mechanical schema/registration generation from typed declarations, never to scan arbitrary methods or bypass validation.
acceptance_criteria: A vehicle-style interaction can be added in one owning Haxe subsystem and becomes available, as authorized, to the CaxeFlow grammar/canonical writer, editor palette and validation, cutscene sequencer, console help/completion, deterministic trace, and CaxeTest locators/actions without duplicating its argument schema or execution semantics; stable IDs and typed arguments round-trip through CaxeMap; unknown actions, wrong arguments, unavailable target objects, duplicate IDs, forbidden consumer authority, and stale editor/test references fail with exact source or object context; gameplay actions execute through ordinary engine systems rather than a DSL-only simulator; CaxeTest player steps continue to use normal semantic input and cannot claim direct action/cheat setup as player-path E2E evidence; cutscenes reuse ordinary game actions and add only bounded presentation commands with explicit skip/final-state behavior; Eval and generated-C parity plus focused native/editor evidence cover registration, parsing, execution, tracing, serialization, and failure; any macro proposal includes a before/after boilerplate inventory and is rejected if an explicit registry remains clearer.
notes: Planning note from the maintainer conversation: this is the extensibility seam for future vehicles and other engine capabilities. It also untangles the apparent overlap between cutscene playback and Playwright-style testing: both consume the engine driver and deterministic clock, but a cutscene is authored game content that sequences actions, while a test supplies input and independent expectations. Coordinate with haxe_c-xge.19.8 and haxe_c-xge.20.3 when implemented; refine together with the maintainer if the first concrete vehicle/cutscene slice reveals broader authority or schema choices.

title: Complete general Haxe class-instance semantics with efficient C representations
description:
## Why

haxe.c already emits concrete private structs, bounded stack construction, instance methods, inheritance, and closed-world virtual dispatch, but that admitted slice is not full Haxe class support. Ordinary Haxe programs and Caxecraft must be able to use classes naturally—including long-lived and escaping instances—without being rewritten into records and static functions merely because the target is C.

## What

Integrate the remaining class semantics into one evidence-backed ordinary-Haxe path: construction and field initialization, inheritance and super, identity and nullability, instance/static methods and properties, interfaces, overrides and dispatch, closures capturing instances, closed generic classes, escaping references, allocation and collection, exceptions and cleanup, reachable reflection/dynamic behavior, and supported finalization policy. Unsupported corners remain explicit until their prerequisites land; this issue is the integration owner, not permission to bypass the E3/E4 feature owners.

## How

Preserve Haxe semantics first, then select the least costly correct C representation from whole-program facts. Proven nonescaping final objects may use automatic storage or scalar replacement. Ordinary non-polymorphic instances use readable private structs and typed functions. Closed calls devirtualize. Only reachable polymorphic hierarchies receive minimal tables. Escaping or cyclic instances use the selected precise managed allocation path and exact roots; native/manual ownership stays explicit. Unused descriptors, tables, reflection, collector features, and cleanup machinery remain absent. Split output keeps recognizable module/type/method ownership and source-shaped names rather than collapsing objects into an opaque runtime API.
acceptance_criteria: Representative ordinary-Haxe programs cover constructors, mutable fields, properties, inheritance/super, identity/null, final/direct and virtual methods, interfaces, closed generic classes, escaping/returned/stored/passed instances, cycles, captured this, exceptions/cleanup, and the admitted reflection/dynamic subset with exact Eval differential behavior; every class/allocation/call site records its representation, escape/lifetime proof, dispatch choice, header/descriptor need, ownership, cleanup, and runtime feature reason; generated split C uses readable structs and typed functions, direct calls where proven, minimal tables only where required, no void-pointer erasure, and no generic object-runtime wrapper for direct cases; nonescaping and scalar-replaced cases allocate zero heap bytes, escaping/cyclic cases use the precise collector with exact roots, and native/manual ownership remains explicit; generated C is warning-clean, sanitizer-clean, deterministic, C++-header consumable where applicable, and measured for compiler time, C compile time, code size, runtime, allocations, and dispatch overhead against reviewed hand-written C baselines; Caxecraft exercises both value-oriented simulation and genuine long-lived entity/session class instances without application-only compiler workarounds; upstream Haxe class tests feed the parity dashboard with no silent skips, and capability documentation lists every remaining unsupported form until the 1.0 semantic gate passes.
notes: 2026-07-21 Caxecraft aquatics pressure: the first design passed a local stack-allocated WaterSimulation instance into a helper, and haxe.c correctly failed closed with TNew(stack-reference-escape:static-call-argument). The callee only needed a read-only water-byte view, so the implementation introduced stateless WaterCellCodec module functions and kept WaterSimulation as the mutable scheduler owner. No raw C or game-specific escape workaround was added. General synchronous borrows and truly escaping class instances remain owned here.

title: Prefer module functions for stateless Haxe namespaces
description:
## Why

Caxecraft and compiler code currently use many classes that exist only to hold static functions. Haxe has supported module-level fields since 4.2, and both the reviewed 4.3.7 source and haxe.c's pinned 5.0.0-preview.1 represent them as KModuleFields. Wrapper classes therefore add ceremony and can make ordinary Haxe look more Java-like than necessary. The rule remains evidence-based because haxe.c has not yet declared module-field lowering as an explicit supported source shape.

## What

Add focused haxe.c evidence for module-level functions and constants, then audit stateless static-only helper classes and migrate the clear cases. Keep real classes when values need per-instance state, identity, inheritance/interface behavior, constructor/lifetime ownership, or an intentional nominal API boundary. Do not mechanically convert entry-point adapters, generated bindings, macro/compiler API requirements, or types whose class identity is part of a public contract.

## How

First add a minimal positive/negative/structural fixture proving pinned Haxe module fields enter the normalized typed program as KModuleFields and lower through the ordinary reachable static-function/global pipeline. Then migrate Caxecraft and compiler helpers in small ownership-focused slices, preserving generated behavior and readable split C. Record temporary exceptions with the missing capability rather than keeping static-only classes by habit.
acceptance_criteria: A focused production CustomTarget(c) fixture proves module-level functions, inline functions where admitted, private helpers, and supported module constants under Eval/C/native output; typed AST and generated-C snapshots show deterministic module ownership and readable names; unsupported module-field forms fail with source-positioned diagnostics and no plausible output; AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md state the evidence-bounded style rule; a reviewed inventory classifies static-only types as migrate, retain for a named semantic/API reason, generated, or blocked by a compiler gap; representative Caxecraft stateless helpers are migrated without behavior, allocation, runtime-feature, or output-layout regressions; stateful entities/sessions continue to use real class instances where their lifetime and identity make that model clearer.
notes:
The local Haxe reference checkout is exact tag 4.3.7 at e0b355c6be312c1b17382603f018cf52522ec651. Its parser/type loader and upstream Haxe Foundation documentation confirm module fields use an implicit KModuleFields static container. haxe.c's normalized typed input already names that class kind and its static graph scans static fields without excluding it, but a production CustomTarget(c) fixture is still required before claiming the source shape and beginning broad migration.
2026-07-21 priority: this is the bounded compiler-quality detour before resuming Caxecraft localization/editor work. Sequence: add focused module-field production evidence; make module ownership/naming/emission explicit; migrate a small representative stateless helper set; run focused generated-C/native gates; then return to haxe_c-xge.19.7. General escaping class semantics are owned separately by haxe_c-53k.2 and must not expand this task.
2026-07-21 completed evidence: the registered function-lowering suite now snapshots the module-fields typed declaration, source-facing project plan, semantic-versus-readable symbol table, split header/module/entry C, Eval oracle, strict native C, and a source-positioned output-free optional-argument rejection. Semantic identity remains the full hidden KModuleFields path; only readable C names/project ownership use the source module. Recovery, Mining, and BerryDrop were migrated to module fields with explicit aliased imports, and the reviewed Caxecraft inventory in docs/caxecraft-domain.md classifies the remaining static-only types. Relevant gates passed: function-lowering, static-initialization, symbol-registry, typed-ast (after one transient HTTP 400 rerun), all-sources, Caxecraft gameplay/pilot/domain/playable/editor/scenario-model/scenario-determinism, formatter, typed-boundaries, capability manifest, snapshot catalog, Beads plan, and CI policy. No runtime feature or allocation was introduced; ordinary class initializer naming remained byte-compatible after a broad-name attempt was caught and narrowed by its snapshot.
2026-07-21 follow-on evidence: PlayerPhysics migrated from a static-only class to module functions so land and aquatic controllers share one ordinary resolveVelocity function. The Caxecraft symbol projection now records both Haxe's full hidden KModuleFields semantic identity and the source-facing readable module symbol; generated C remains named hxc_caxecraft_domain_PlayerPhysics_*. Eval/native behavior, runtime-free output, and maintainability gates pass.
close_reason: Module-level functions now have focused compiler evidence, readable C naming, negative coverage, documented policy/inventory, and representative Caxecraft migrations.

title: Add a typed in-game console and agent control bridge
description:
## Why

Caxecraft needs an attractive console for debugging, Creative cheats, test authoring, and autonomous agent investigation. Separate hidden debug functions would drift from CaxeTest and make failures harder to reproduce. Arbitrary code evaluation or an always-open network console would be unsafe.

## What

Add one typed engine-command dispatcher used by a RayguiHx console, CaxeTest actions, and an explicitly enabled local agent-control protocol. Commands resolve stable campaign, level, entry, object, entity, item, objective, message, and test IDs through the loaded content model.

## How

Parse console text into a closed GameCommand enum with exact diagnostics, permission class, deterministic tick-boundary execution, and structured results. Keep invariant ASCII command names for reproducible scripts while localizing help, descriptions, feedback, and completion labels. Separate ordinary player commands, Creative/cheat commands, developer inspection commands, and test-only arrange operations. No command evaluates Haxe/C/Lua, invokes a shell, reads arbitrary files, opens the network, or obtains raw pointers.

The first agent bridge is disabled by default and activated only by an explicit automation launch mode. Prefer a bounded local request/response transport with an unguessable per-run token, structured JSONL messages, no remote bind, and deterministic trace/screenshot artifacts. It drives the same command and input services as the visible console and CaxeTest rather than manipulating process memory.
acceptance_criteria: A documented closed GameCommand model and parser cover help/completion, inspect player/world/object/rule, teleport to admitted entry/object, give/remove admitted items, heal/set bounded player resources, toggle declared Creative movement aids, spawn/remove admitted entities, set/reset declared objective/object state, load an admitted campaign level/entry, save/load through normal persistence, capture a named screenshot/trace, and list/run/step CaxeTest cases; every command has a permission class, typed arguments/results, source/console diagnostic, fixed-tick execution rule, undo/save/audit effect, and deterministic bounded cost; the RayguiHx console has styled scrollback, history, completion from loaded typed IDs, visible focus, English/Mexican-Spanish help, keyboard/controller close and navigation, safe text capacity, and no steady-state allocation regression; cheats require an explicit world/session setting and visibly mark the session/save, developer/test commands require an explicit development or automation profile, and test-only arrange commands never count as player-path E2E evidence; the agent bridge is off by default, local-only, per-run authenticated, bounded in message size/rate/time, exposes no arbitrary filesystem/network/code authority, and shuts down cleanly; the agent can launch, query, drive input/commands, wait by game ticks, run CaxeTest, capture screenshots/traces, and quit without human assistance; console, CaxeTest, and agent requests all reach the same engine dispatcher and observable state, with Eval/generated-C/native parity and sanitizer evidence.

comments.author: Marcelo Serpa
comments.text: Transport refinement: prefer a versioned, length-bounded protocol over inherited Haxe standard input/output when the agent runner launches the game. This avoids ports, firewall rules, remote binding, discovery, and socket lifecycle while supporting two-way commands, observations, screenshots, and clean EOF. Use a loopback socket only if attach-to-running-process or multi-client requirements later justify its security/platform cost. All in-game encoder/parser/dispatcher code remains Haxe compiled through haxe.c; host orchestration may be Python but cannot duplicate game semantics. The issue now depends on haxe_c-8al for haxe.io streams and haxe_c-0bx if JSON Lines remains the selected spelling.

title: Build CaxeTest into the engine as a Playwright-style game driver
description:
## Why

Caxecraft needs fast tests that play authored content through the real engine. Hand-written Haxe probes do not give map authors a public testing path, while bespoke test simulators can drift from the game and native full-game replays are too slow for every rule edit.

## What

Add a bounded data-only CaxeTest DSL and an engine-owned game-driver API. A test launches a campaign or standalone level, finds UI and world elements by stable semantic IDs, drives the ordinary input/gameplay path, waits in bounded game ticks, and asserts visible or semantic outcomes. The same typed Haxe parser and driver run headlessly on Eval, through generated C, and inside the native Raylib game/editor.

## How

The driver uses the real GameSession, semantic input mapping, fixed-step update, collision/gameplay systems, CaxeFlow, cutscenes, level loader, saves, and read-only observations. It must not reimplement those systems for tests. Playwright-like locators name menus, controls, actors, objects, zones, levels, messages, and objectives without fragile screen coordinates. Explicit arrange/setup operations may create a focused starting state, but reports distinguish them from player actions and they never count as end-to-end evidence.

The editor derives valid selectors and test skeletons from the open campaign/level, records semantic actions during test play, lets an author insert independent assertions, runs or steps a case, shows traces/screenshots, and navigates a failure back to its referenced object, rule, or message. Generated expectations are prohibited: a bad map rule cannot write its own passing oracle.
acceptance_criteria: CAXETEST 1 has a documented bounded scalar-UTF-8 grammar, canonical writer, nominal locators, exact source diagnostics, and malformed-input corpus; the game engine exposes one typed driver used by Eval, generated C, the native Raylib executable, and the in-game editor without a parallel test simulation; tests can launch a standalone level or campaign entry, choose locale/mode/seed, select semantic UI/world locators, press/hold/release mapped actions, look or move through the normal player path, interact/use/select, advance or wait for bounded fixed ticks, and request screenshots; assertions cover visible UI/message/loading state, current campaign/level/entry, player position bounds, inventory, health/air, object/quest/objective state, dialogue/journal/cutscene facts, emitted audio/effects, save state, and expected diagnostics; automatic waits have explicit tick budgets and failures report case, step, locator, tick, relevant state difference, and available screenshot/trace; the editor presents campaign > level > region/object/rule/test navigation, generates selectors and skeletons, records semantic actions, inserts independently authored assertions, runs/steps cases, and jumps failures to source; typed rename/delete updates or rejects test references; normal and skipped village departure, Nia interaction, movement/collision, block edit, Tideweave route, bridge puzzle, save/reload, and a bounded boss-state case pass on Eval and generated C, with selected native UI/render cases; arrange/setup steps are visibly classified and never count as player-path E2E evidence; invalid/stale locators, arbitrary code/paths, unbounded waits, unavailable presentation assertions, and exceeded budgets fail closed; native pilots remain the focused authority for platform/window/GPU behavior rather than duplicating the complete campaign.
notes: The Playwright-style engine driver owns the shared typed command/input/observation boundary. haxe_c-xge.19.8.1 adds the visible game console and explicitly enabled agent bridge over that boundary. Console cheats and test arrange operations are classified in traces and cannot be reported as normal player-path evidence.

title: Add a pinned typed RayguiHx surface for Caxecraft UI
description:
## Why

Caxecraft needs an intuitive editor and consistent menus, settings, pause, inventory, dialogs, and loading controls. raygui is a small immediate-mode GUI designed for raylib tools, but calling its C header directly throughout game code would spread pointer, result-code, lifetime, styling, and layout details through the showcase.

## What

Pin one reviewed raygui release and bind only the controls Caxecraft uses. Provide an ABI-faithful raygui.raw layer plus a small ergonomic Haxe facade with typed results, state owners, responsive layout helpers, Caxecraft styling, localization-safe text, and semantic input/focus behavior. Keep the raw layer usable by other haxe.c programs and grow it gradually from executable demand.

## How

Start from raygui 5.0 tag commit 020a61bebcbe288b4414de3416e219ef40af847a and header SHA-256 9428525eef081eaec45f8fedbb68199bcbe876f2ce927d5e2a69e811a01ae3a9, but admit it only after full pinned-raylib 6.0 native and runtime probes. Compile RAYGUI_IMPLEMENTATION in exactly one owned translation unit. Model controls, styles, result flags, edited values, and text buffers with closed typed Haxe surfaces; do not expose general raw pointers as the ergonomic API. Use raygui for tool/panel UI and keep the crosshair, hotbar, health, target feedback, and other always-visible stylized HUD elements on the direct raylib renderer unless measurement proves a better boundary.
acceptance_criteria: An immutable lock records raygui source revision, archive/header hashes, license, provenance, exact selected declarations, pinned raylib compatibility, and one implementation owner; Clang/GCC and C/C++ native consumers compile and run the selected raw ABI against the pinned raylib 6.0 build with warnings enabled; generated Haxe calls the selected raygui functions directly with no raw C injection, reflection, hxrt, or steady-state allocation; the semantic facade represents result flags, mutable control state, text capacity/lifetime, style properties, localization, focus, and layout with typed owners and actionable misuse diagnostics; menus, settings, pause, loading UI, inventory panels, and the editor can share the facade and Caxecraft style while the gameplay HUD boundary is documented; keyboard, mouse, and controller navigation, visible focus, viewport scaling, English/Mexican-Spanish text, long labels, and cleanup have semantic and real-render evidence; only used controls are bound and adding a control requires regenerated lock/snapshot/native evidence; upstream zlib notice and any repository modification are recorded accurately.
notes: Compiler/interop mandate: RayguiHx is also a product-level foreign-library proof for haxe.c. The lane must provision an immutable third-party header, generate/review a selected raw ABI surface, compile the implementation macro in exactly one project-owned C translation unit, call it from ordinary Haxe beside existing RaylibHx and Caxecraft modules, and show readable split/unity/package C plus native parity. Any missing header-only-library, pointer/out-parameter, result-bit, mutable text-buffer, macro-build, or multi-library link capability becomes a minimal compiler/binding regression and general fix; no Caxecraft-only raw C or compiler path special case is allowed.

title: Author simple data-driven Caxecraft cutscenes
description:
## Why

Adventure needs appealing story moments without hard-coded game-loop branches, pre-rendered video, or a general cinematic engine. The same public authoring path should let a child or designer introduce Haxirio, Ivvy, Ceesh, Browser, important locations, and level transitions.

## What

Add a small typed cutscene sequence to CaxeFlow and the editor. A cutscene coordinates a bounded set of camera shots, actor positions/facing, localized dialogue or narration, waits, sound/music requests, simple fades, state/objective changes, and an optional level transition.

## How

Keep simulation and persistent outcomes deterministic while presentation interpolates smoothly. Each cutscene declares how controls, pause, advance, replay, and skip behave. Skipping applies the same required final story and world state exactly once, then returns control at a declared safe point. Reuse ordinary CaxeFlow actions where possible and add closed cinematic actions only when the existing vocabulary cannot express the presentation.
acceptance_criteria: The editor can create, order, preview, validate, save, reload, and test a cutscene without editing Haxe; the first village departure and at least one Browser/Ceesh story moment use the public cutscene model; admitted actions cover localized cards/dialogue, camera shot or movement, actor facing or bounded movement, fixed-tick wait or explicit player advance, fade, typed audio request, state/objective change, and optional level transition; keyboard, mouse, and controller can advance, pause, replay, and skip according to declared policy; skipping and normal completion produce the same required persistent state exactly once and cannot strand the player, duplicate rewards, or leave controls/camera locked; subtitles and essential visual cues work in English and Mexican Spanish and do not depend on sound or color alone; invalid actor, camera marker, message, audio, level, or entry references fail before play; Eval/C traces and native pilots cover normal, skip, replay, interruption, save/reload, and cleanup; no authored cutscene contains arbitrary code or host paths.

comments.author: Marcelo Serpa
comments.text: Mini-movie design clarification: cutscenes are ordinary actions launched by any compatible CaxeFlow event. The focused editor owns named camera anchors/targets, actor staging markers, ordered beats, bounded parallel camera/actor/audio lanes, cuts/holds/blends/paths/follow shots, field of view, dialogue/subtitles, fades, choices, and persistent commit beats. Normal and skip paths converge through one idempotent completion batch and restore controls/cursor/camera/audio. Reduced-motion substitutes, missing-reference validation, preview of normal and skipped final state, and Pilot/CaxeTest input-driven checks are required. This remains a bounded data model, not a separate trigger language or unrestricted cinematic scripting VM.

title: Add multi-level Caxecraft campaigns and typed level transitions
description:
## Why

Caxecraft needs independently loadable levels as well as seamless regions. A single CaxeMap world is ideal for a Creative map or compact scenario, but a campaign, challenge collection, or user-created minigame pack may need several worlds with explicit entrances and exits.

## What

Define a small campaign package that names CaxeMap levels and connects typed exits to typed entrances. Keep each .caxemap valid and playable on its own where its declared requirements permit. Support shared campaign progress without merging all terrain into one enormous world.

## How

Distinguish the concepts clearly: chunks are technical voxel units; regions are seamless named areas within one level; a level is one independently loadable CaxeMap world; a campaign is a validated graph of levels. Add only the transition, persistence, editor, validation, and packaging surface required by real Caxecraft content. Do not create a general scene engine.
acceptance_criteria: A campaign can declare multiple stable level IDs, each resolving to one validated logical CaxeMap path; typed exits name an existing destination level and entrance, and missing or cyclic-required progression fails with actionable diagnostics; entering a level installs its world atomically and places the player at the declared entrance without carrying stale level-local state; campaign/player/quest state and level-local state have explicit persistence rules; saves record current level plus required per-level changes and reject campaign/version mismatch without corrupting known-good state; the editor can create, rename safely, connect, validate, test, save, and reload levels and transitions; headless traces cover linear, branching, return, missing destination, retry, and save/reload paths; native pilots cover at least one visible transition and return; one-level Creative maps remain simple and do not require campaign boilerplate.
notes: 2026-07-21 campaign shape: the reference Adventure begins in a compact village level, then the village exit performs an explicit loading transition into the definitive broad outdoor level. Level changes use a branded accessible loading screen that names the destination, keeps the window responsive, reports real bounded progress where available, permits quit, and never displays fake progress. Visual assets belong to the visual pipeline; transition state and cleanup belong to the typed level loader.

title: Build Caxecraft's broad explorable authored world
description:
## Why

Caxecraft should grow beyond the compact first playable into a world that feels large, surprising, and enjoyable to explore. Size alone is not enough: empty travel would make the game slower rather than more fun.

## What

Design and implement a broad but finite authored world made from connected regions. Preserve Evergrove, the falls, underwater ruins, Frostmere, Ashfall, the house, castle, and boss route, then add optional landmarks, caves, overlooks, secrets, building sites, encounters, resources, NPC moments, and shortcuts. Keep the critical Adventure route understandable while making exploration and Creative play substantially more open-ended. All shipped terrain and content must use CaxeMap/CaxeFlow and the public editor path.

## How

Use chunked storage and rendering, plus bounded region loading where measurement requires it. Give each region a distinctive silhouette, material palette, traversal idea, audio mood, and useful reward. Favor loops, shortcuts, sightlines, and discoveries over long empty corridors. Keep simulation deterministic and save every relevant world change through the ordinary save model.
acceptance_criteria: A complete authored world is substantially larger than the first-playable meadow and contains all named biomes plus optional regions; every traversable region has at least one landmark and meaningful activity or reward; the main route stays readable through sightlines, journal guidance, and recoverable hints; optional exploration provides secrets, resources, encounters, NPC moments, shortcuts, or good Creative building sites rather than empty distance; travel is paced so playtests do not reveal repeated long stretches without a decision or discovery; terrain, placements, dialogue, objectives, encounters, and progression round-trip through CaxeMap/CaxeFlow and the public editor; chunked rendering/loading, saves, deterministic traces, and native pilots cover movement across region boundaries without pop-in, lost edits, stalls, or unbounded memory growth; finite limits and deliberate non-goals are documented without describing the first playable as the full world.

title: Author localized scenario text in CaxeMap and the editor
description: Extend the public CaxeMap/editor path so scenario authors define available locale IDs, one default locale, and whole translated messages by stable MessageId. Dialogue, objectives, journal entries, tutorials, and CaxeFlow cards reference IDs; gameplay state and saves never store translated prose. The editor edits the same catalog records that the game validates and loads.
design: Add the smallest versioned CAXEMAP extension that preserves canonical bytes and human diffs. Keep locale catalogs beside scenario content, reject missing/duplicate/unknown locale-message pairs, and use exact-locale then documented fallback without cycles. Do not embed arbitrary code, language branches, or a second editor-only format. Native file loading remains dependent on haxe_c-xge.19.4; pure model/codec/editor commands may proceed independently where bounded.
acceptance_criteria: The grammar/model/writer/parser represent scenario locale inventory, default locale, and whole messages; canonical load-save-load is byte-identical; editor commands can add/remove a locale and edit one complete translation while validation prevents orphaned required message IDs; one compact bilingual fixture drives representative dialogue through the shared lookup; malformed/fallback cases have focused tests; the shipped scenario path contains no campaign prose that exists only in Haxe source.
notes: Completed 2026-07-21. The shipped first-playable campaign now uses scenarios/first-playable/map.caxemap as the single canonical home for its map, story references, locale inventory, default locale, and complete en/es-mx message catalogs. The temporary messages.json sidecar was removed. The typed parser/model/writer validates exact translation sets, unresolved message IDs, duplicate locales/messages, fallback behavior, canonical order, and byte-identical load-save-load. Editor commands add/remove locales, choose the default, and add/update/remove complete translations through normal validated undo/redo history. A narrow generated Haxe rendering adapter is derived from the CaxeMap only because native String/Bytes/filesystem loading remains tracked by haxe_c-xge.19.4; gameplay selects typed message IDs and contains no language branches. Fixtures moved under test/fixtures/caxemap so shipped campaign content is separate from parser test data. Verified with test:caxecraft-scenario-model, test:caxecraft-localization, test:caxecraft-editor, test:caxecraft-scenario-determinism, test:caxecraft-playable, format:haxe:check, test:typed-boundaries, and test:beads-plan. Next is the shared typed action registry haxe_c-xge.19.9, then engine-integrated CaxeTest haxe_c-xge.19.8.
close_reason: Embedded localized campaign content, editor workflow, canonical codec, generated native adapter, and focused/playable verification are complete.

title: Decouple playable UI text into locale catalogs
description: Remove English/Spanish branching and player-visible prose from Caxecraft Main and TitleMenu. Store the built-in UI copy in a closed, validated UTF-8 JSON catalog and generate typed message IDs plus the narrow C/raylib text adapter required by today's literal-only CString boundary. The application selects a locale cursor and asks for message IDs; it does not know which locales exist.
design: JSON is the reviewable source of truth. A deterministic generator validates stable IDs, unique locale IDs, one default, complete translations, valid scalar UTF-8, no embedded NUL, bounded text, and exact inventory, then emits typed Haxe. The generated C adapter keeps each borrowed c.CString at a direct literal call so lifetime remains static. This is build-time embedding, clearly documented as an interim delivery shape until E5 String/Bytes/filesystem can load external catalogs. Do not add Dynamic JSON to gameplay, language-specific branches, sentence fragments, reflection, raw C, or a general localization framework.
acceptance_criteria: Main.hx and TitleMenu.hx contain no GameLanguage type, English/Spanish identities, locale comparisons, or player-visible prose literals; one catalog-data change can add a locale without editing application code; changing the locale cursor immediately changes menu/HUD/dialogue text without restart or gameplay-state changes; catalog validation has focused positive and malformed/missing/duplicate/NUL tests; generated output is deterministic, typed, direct-literal CString-safe, allocation/hxrt/raw-injection free, and snapshot-tested; existing English and es-MX visual pilots plus playable generated-C/resource gates pass. Full font coverage, runtime file loading, editor UI, and complete Adventure translation remain explicitly open under haxe_c-xge.21/.19.
notes: Completed the playable decoupling. Main/TitleMenu now carry only LocaleCursor and typed UiMessage/ScenarioMessage values; GameLanguage and all language branches/direct prose draws were removed. locales/ui.json owns generic UI and scenarios/first-playable/messages.json owns Nia/story content. Strict validation covers exact schema, duplicate JSON keys/IDs/locales, missing translations, scalar/control/NUL bounds, deterministic generated Haxe, app-source policy, and exact native packaging. Generated split C snapshots retain 70 direct static-lifetime DrawText literals in two dedicated modules, no goto/allocation/hxrt, and Main shrank by delegating presentation. A general compiler fix plus aggregate regression admits Int-backed enum-abstract switch subjects. English launch-smoke and secondary-locale Spanish title pilots both presented 1280x720 framebuffers and exited within 15 seconds. Full font/diacritic coverage, runtime catalog loading, editor authoring, and complete campaign translation remain under parent haxe_c-xge.21 and haxe_c-xge.19.7.
close_reason: Locale-blind application, split UI/scenario catalogs, validation/generation/packaging, exact C snapshots, and both graphical locale pilots are complete.

title: Render the base world from the terrain atlas
description: Replace solid-color DrawCube terrain in the first playable with exposed textured faces from the reviewed base terrain atlas. Add the smallest pinned/Clang-verified rlgl binding needed to submit all visible quads through raylib's default render batch, stage one exact atlas owner, draw a simple sun and atmospheric distance tint, and render the complete current 32x32 world so walking cannot cross a moving radius boundary.
design: This is a bounded vertical step toward chunk meshes, not a second engine. The renderer receives World cells and maps BlockKind to typed face roles; no map geometry or actor placement belongs here. One rlSetTexture/rlBegin/rlEnd region emits only exposed faces with atlas UVs and direction lighting. The current finite world can be fully visible; larger CaxeMap worlds remain owned by the chunk task. Validate RL_QUADS and seven selected rlgl declarations from the pinned header, and keep the semantic facade narrow.
acceptance_criteria: The base terrain atlas is staged byte-for-byte, checked, drawn, and unloaded once; Grass uses distinct top/side/bottom cells while Dirt, Stone, and Bedrock use reviewed cells; generated C directly calls the selected rlgl API with no raw injection, hxrt, steady-state allocation, or per-block DrawCube; the complete current 32x32 world has no moving render-radius boundary; sky/sun and distance tint remain visible. Keep evidence proportional: one focused typed face/UV test, one moving real-Raylib pilot, the existing playable generated-C/resource gate, and the precise binding/native ABI check.
notes: Implemented the bounded full-world terrain slice. The game now packages the reviewed 4x4 terrain atlas, maps BlockKind plus voxel face to typed atlas cells, emits exposed faces in one allocation-free rlgl quad region, keeps the selection outline separate, draws a calm screen-space sun, and removes the moving nine-block render boundary. The raw rlgl surface is generated from a closed seven-function selection and pinned Clang-derived lock with a native ABI probe. The real move-jump-edit pilot presented a textured 1280x720 framebuffer and exited within its 15-second window; playable snapshots/direct-C checks, the C and es_MX atlas probe, asset policy, typed-boundary policy, CI policy, snapshot catalog, formatter, and raylib provisioning checks passed. The inline vertex helper remains deliberately non-inline because haxe_c-djl.2 owns the general lost-Float-coercion compiler bug; larger cached chunk meshes remain with the parent terrain roadmap.
close_reason: Full current-world terrain atlas rendering, narrow verified rlgl binding, generated-C snapshots, focused atlas semantics, real graphical pilot, and policy documentation are complete. Larger-world chunk caching remains in the parent roadmap; the discovered inline Float conversion bug is tracked separately as haxe_c-djl.2.

title: Make guarded Beads history scan compatible with nullable legacy records
description: After the reviewed Beads 1.1.0 migration, npm run beads:push stops after the current-record gitleaks scan. bd history haxe_c-fbq --json exits 1 because a historical description column is NULL and the CLI tries to scan it into a non-null string. scripts/security/run-beads-gitleaks.sh is fail-closed and therefore cannot scan the remaining 227 issue histories or publish audited Dolt state.
design: Preserve fail-closed scanning; do not skip unreadable histories or bypass push-safe. Determine whether the correct owner is a reviewed Beads upgrade, a one-time audited data normalization that retains historical meaning, or an upstream-compatible history export path. Add a fixture containing the nullable legacy shape and prove current plus complete historical records reach gitleaks before push.
acceptance_criteria: The focused nullable-history fixture passes; npm run beads:push scans all current records and all issue histories, prints the explicit OK and push phases, and pushes or reports a genuine remote error; no history is silently omitted or rewritten without audited evidence; the reviewed binary lock and migration documentation remain consistent.
notes: 2026-07-21 after localization closure: npm run beads:push still scans 232 current/history records successfully with no detected leaks, then exits 1 before printing the guarded wrapper's 'Pushing the audited Dolt history' line. No push was attempted and the guard was not bypassed. The failure remains in the history-scan command boundary and needs the reviewed Beads tooling fix.

title: Texture the voxel world and remove distance pop-in
description: Turn the current nearby solid-color cube loop into a coherent original terrain presentation. Integrate the reviewed terrain atlas through typed raylib bindings, render exposed voxel faces with material-specific top/side/bottom cells, add an original sky/sun/horizon treatment, and coordinate with the chunked-rendering owner so a longer view distance does not restore per-block draw-call stalls. The visible boundary must fade or blend into atmosphere instead of sharply appearing and disappearing as the player walks.
design: Keep world state and material choice renderer-independent. Build reusable chunk/material geometry or the smallest equivalent bounded batch representation; do not solve the symptom by only increasing RENDER_RADIUS. Keep direct inspectable raylib calls, explicit resource ownership, no steady-state allocation, deterministic pilot evidence, and readable generated C. Treat the camera field-of-view angle separately from terrain view distance: tune both deliberately and name them clearly.
acceptance_criteria: Grass, dirt, stone, wood, leaves, sand, snow, water, and ash use reviewed atlas cells with appropriate face roles; sky, sun, and horizon treatment are visible in representative pilots; walking no longer causes a sharp nearby terrain boundary; draw calls/frame time/allocations stay within recorded budgets at the admitted view distance; generated C and resource cleanup remain direct, warning-clean, hxrt-free, and regression-tested; focused semantic plus real-raylib screenshot evidence covers material mapping, view-distance transition, resize, and bounded exit.
notes: Renderer integration must accept material IDs and visible cells from the world/scenario boundary. It may use a small deterministic fixture while native CaxeMap loading remains blocked, but it must not encode campaign geometry, placements, biomes, or progression in renderer code. Terrain atlas mapping is reusable presentation policy; authored map content remains owned by haxe_c-xge.19/.20.

title: Render Nia and Mossling from the original entity atlas
description: Replace the temporary code-drawn Nia and Mossling silhouettes with the reviewed original entity atlas in the real C/raylib game. Add only the precise DrawBillboardRec raw binding required by this presentation slice, extend the existing Texture2D borrow contract, stage the exact pinned atlas byte, and retain explicit load/validity/unload ownership. Gameplay state remains renderer-independent and the code-drawn path remains the invalid-texture fallback.
design: Use one typed atlas-cell mapping and one Caxecraft texture adapter. Draw camera-facing world sprites from row-major manifest cells, keep combat telegraph and drop feedback structural, and use semantic screenshot evidence rather than GPU-wide byte goldens. Do not add a generic animation engine, resource registry, raw C, or target-specific gameplay branch.
acceptance_criteria: The pinned core binding extracts and ABI-probes DrawBillboardRec; its Texture2D borrow contract and generated raw Haxe are current; Caxecraft stages, validates, borrows, and unloads the entity atlas exactly once; Nia and living Mossling render from typed cells with an explicit code-drawn fallback; native screenshots prove each original actor through atlas-specific semantic pixels, combat defeat still removes Mossling, and the normal/pilot generated C remains direct, warning-clean, allocation/hxrt/goto free with release snapshots, asset gates, and cleanup counts passing.
notes: Implemented and verified the exact pinned DrawBillboardRec binding, Texture2D non-retaining borrow contract, five-asset staging and ownership, typed 4x4 WorldSprite selection, and code-drawn invalid-texture fallback.
